channel.title: Nullable on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions
channel.description: Recent content in Nullable on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions

item.title: [Solution] Dart Null Check Operator Used on Null Value — Runtime Error Fix
item.description:
Null Check Operator Used on Null Value — Runtime Error Fix

A Null check operator used on a null value error occurs when you use the ! (null check) operator on a variable that is actually null. This is a runtime exception in Dart’s null safety system.

Description

In Dart’s null-safe mode, the ! operator asserts that a value is non-null and forces it into a non-nullable type. If the value is null at runtime, Dart throws a LateInitializationError or a TypeError with the message “Null check operator used on a null value”.

item.title: [Solution] Kotlin NullPointerException — Null Reference Fix
item.description:
NullPointerException — Null Reference Fix

A NullPointerException is thrown when you attempt to dereference an object reference that is null. Kotlin’s type system is designed to eliminate these, but they can still occur in certain situations.

Description

Kotlin distinguishes between nullable and non-nullable types at compile time. However, NPEs can still happen when using platform types from Java, the !! operator, uninitialized lateinit variables, or untrusted data.

Common scenarios:

  • Using the !! operator on a null value — force-unwrapping a nullable reference.
  • Java interop — receiving a null from Java code into a non-nullable Kotlin type.
  • Accessing an uninitialized lateinit variable — calling methods before assignment.
  • External JSON or config data — values that may be absent at runtime.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Force-unwrap with !!
val name: String? = null
println(name!!.length) // NullPointerException

// Cause 2: Java interop returning null into non-nullable type
val javaList: JavaList<String> = getJavaList() // Java method may return null
val first: String = javaList.get(0) // NullPointerException if list is null

// Cause 3: Uninitialized lateinit property
class User {
 lateinit var name: String
 fun greet() = println("Hello, $name")
}
val user = User()
user.greet() // UninitializedPropertyAccessException (subclass of NPE)

// Cause 4: Unsafe cast of null
val data: Any? = null
val str: String = data as String // ClassCastException, but can also trigger NPE

Solutions

Fix 1: Use safe call operator ?.

// Wrong
val name: String? = null
println(name!!.length) // NullPointerException

// Correct
println(name?.length) // Prints null, no exception

Fix 2: Use the Elvis operator ?: for defaults

// Wrong
val name: String? = null
val length = name!!.length // NullPointerException

// Correct
val length = name?.length ?: 0 // Returns 0 if name is null

Fix 3: Use let blocks for scoped null checks

// Wrong
val name: String? = getName()
println(name!!.uppercase()) // NullPointerException if null

// Correct
name?.let {
 println(it.uppercase()) // Only executes if name is not null
} ?: println("Name is null")

Fix 4: Check lateinit before use

// Wrong
class User {
 lateinit var name: String
 fun greet() = println("Hello, $name")
}

// Correct
class User {
 lateinit var name: String
 fun greet() {
 if (::name.isInitialized) {
 println("Hello, $name")
 } else {
 println("Name not set")
 }
 }
}

Fix 5: Handle Java nulls explicitly

// Wrong — trusting Java to never return null
val result: String = javaMethod() // May throw NPE

// Correct
val result: String? = javaMethod()
val safeResult = result ?: "default"

Examples

fun main() {
 val nullable: String? = null

 // This will throw NullPointerException
 // println(nullable!!.length)

 // Safe alternatives
 println(nullable?.length) // null
 println(nullable?.length ?: -1) // -1

 nullable?.let {
 println(it.length)
 } ?: println("Value was null")
}

Related Errors

  • ClassCastException — wrong type cast at runtime.
  • UninitializedPropertyAccessException — accessing lateinit before initialization.
  • IllegalArgumentException — invalid argument passed to a method.

item.title: [Solution] Kotlin NullPointerException — Null Safety Fix
item.description:
NullPointerException — Null Safety Fix

A NullPointerException occurs when you attempt to dereference a null reference, bypassing Kotlin’s null safety system.

Description

Kotlin’s type system distinguishes between nullable and non-nullable types at compile time. However, NPEs can still occur when using the !! operator, Java interop, or uninitialized lateinit variables.

Common causes:

  • Force unwrap with !! — explicitly allowing null dereference
  • Java interop — receiving null from Java into non-nullable type
  • Uninitialized lateinit — accessing before assignment
  • Platform types — null from external sources

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Force unwrap with !!
val name: String? = null
println(name!!.length) // NullPointerException

// Cause 2: Java interop returning null
val javaList: JavaList<String> = getJavaList()
val first: String = javaList.get(0) // NPE if list is null

// Cause 3: Uninitialized lateinit
class User {
 lateinit var name: String
 fun greet() = println("Hello, $name")
}
val user = User()
user.greet() // UninitializedPropertyAccessException

// Cause 4: Unsafe cast
val data: Any? = null
val str: String = data as String // ClassCastException/NPE

How to Fix

Fix 1: Use safe call operator ?.

// Wrong
val name: String? = null
println(name!!.length) // NPE

// Correct
println(name?.length) // null, no exception

Fix 2: Use Elvis operator ?:

// Wrong
val name: String? = null
val length = name!!.length // NPE

// Correct
val length = name?.length ?: 0 // 0

Fix 3: Use let blocks

// Wrong
val name: String? = getName()
println(name!!.uppercase()) // NPE

// Correct
name?.let {
 println(it.uppercase())
} ?: println("Name is null")

Fix 4: Check lateinit before use

// Wrong
class User {
 lateinit var name: String
 fun greet() = println("Hello, $name")
}

// Correct
class User {
 lateinit var name: String
 fun greet() {
 if (::name.isInitialized) {
 println("Hello, $name")
 } else {
 println("Name not set")
 }
 }
}

Examples

// Example 1: Safe null handling
fun processUser(user: User?) {
 user?.let {
 println(it.name)
 it.address?.let { addr ->
 println(addr.city)
 }
 } ?: println("User is null")
}

// Example 2: Default values
val config = mapOf("host" to "localhost")
val port = config["port"]?.toIntOrNull() ?: 8080

Related Errors

  • ClassCastException — wrong type cast at runtime
  • IllegalArgumentException — invalid argument passed
  • IndexOutOfBoundsException — index beyond collection size

item.title: [Solution] Kotlin Type Mismatch — Compile-Time Type Fix
item.description:
Type Mismatch — Compile-Time Type Fix

A type mismatch error occurs at compile time when Kotlin’s type system detects an incompatible type. This is a compile error, not a runtime exception, and must be fixed before the code can run.

Description

Kotlin has a strict type system that catches type errors during compilation. Type mismatch happens when you assign a value of one type to a variable of a different type without proper conversion.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2531 — Object Is Possibly 'null'
item.description:
TS2531 — Object is possibly ’null'

TS2531 occurs when you access a property or call a method on an object that could be null. This is enforced when strictNullChecks is enabled in your tsconfig, which is the default in modern TypeScript projects.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: DOM query that might return null
const el = document.getElementById("app");
el.innerHTML = "Hello"; // TS2531: Object is possibly 'null'

// Cause 2: Function parameter that accepts null
function process(value: string | null) {
 console.log(value.length); // TS2531
}

// Cause 3: Array.find() returns undefined
const items = [1, 2, 3];
const found = items.find(x => x === 99);
console.log(found.toFixed()); // TS2531 (find returns T | undefined)

// Cause 4: Optional property access without null check
interface Config {
 host?: string;
}
function getHost(config: Config) {
 return config.host.toUpperCase(); // TS2531
}

Solutions

Fix 1: Add a null check before accessing properties

// Wrong
const el = document.getElementById("app");
el.innerHTML = "Hello";

// Correct
const el = document.getElementById("app");
if (el) {
 el.innerHTML = "Hello";
}

Fix 2: Use optional chaining

// Wrong
const el = document.getElementById("app");
el.innerHTML = "Hello";

// Correct
const el = document.getElementById("app");
el?.innerHTML = "Hello";

Fix 3: Use non-null assertion when you’re sure

// Use ! only when you're certain the value is not null
const el = document.getElementById("app")!;
el.innerHTML = "Hello";

Fix 4: Use type narrowing in function parameters

function process(value: string | null) {
 if (value === null) {
 console.log("No value provided");
 return;
 }
 console.log(value.length); // OK — value is string
}

Examples

// Example 1: Document.querySelector returns Element | null
const btn = document.querySelector("button");
btn.addEventListener("click", handler); // TS2531

// Example 2: Object destructuring with optional values
interface Response {
 data?: { name: string };
}
function getName(res: Response) {
 return res.data.name; // TS2531: data is possibly undefined
}

Related Errors

  • TS2532: Object is possibly ‘undefined’ — similar but for undefined instead of null.
  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — broader type assignability issues.
  • TS18046: ‘X’ is of type ‘unknown’ — untyped values need narrowing.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2532 — Object Is Possibly 'undefined'
item.description:
TS2532 — Object is possibly ‘undefined’

TS2532 occurs when you access a property or call a method on a value that could be undefined. With strictNullChecks enabled, TypeScript requires you to verify that a value exists before using it.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Array element access without bounds checking
const arr: number[] = [1, 2, 3];
const value = arr[10];
value.toFixed(); // TS2532: Object is possibly 'undefined'

// Cause 2: Object property access on optional property
interface Config {
 database?: { host: string };
}
function getHost(config: Config) {
 return config.database.host; // TS2532: database is possibly undefined
}

// Cause 3: Map.get() returns V | undefined
const map = new Map<string, number>();
const val = map.get("key");
val.toFixed(); // TS2532

// Cause 4: Function return type includes undefined
function findUser(id: number): User | undefined {
 return users.find(u => u.id === id);
}
const user = findUser(1);
console.log(user.name); // TS2532

Solutions

Fix 1: Use optional chaining

// Wrong
const val = map.get("key");
val.toFixed();

// Correct
const val = map.get("key");
val?.toFixed();

Fix 2: Add a truthiness check

// Wrong
const user = findUser(1);
console.log(user.name);

// Correct
const user = findUser(1);
if (user) {
 console.log(user.name);
}

Fix 3: Use default values with nullish coalescing

const val = map.get("key") ?? 0;
val.toFixed(); // OK — val is number

Fix 4: Use non-null assertion when certain

// Use ! only when you're sure the value exists
const val = map.get("key")!;
val.toFixed();

Examples

// Example 1: Nested optional properties
interface Company {
 ceo?: {
 name?: string;
 };
}
function getCEOName(company: Company) {
 return company.ceo.name; // TS2532: ceo and name are possibly undefined
}
// Fix: company.ceo?.name

// Example 2: Promise.then with possible undefined
Promise.resolve(42).then(result => {
 result.toFixed(); // OK — result is number
});

Related Errors

  • TS2531: Object is possibly ’null’ — similar but for null instead of undefined.
  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — broader type assignability.
  • TS2741: Property is missing in type — related to object shape mismatches.
